source: All-In Podcast — E278: AI memory crunch, Micron blowout, NYC socialists, SpaceX float (w/ Kalanick)
summary: SpaceX IPO lockup short thesis is likely wrong due to prior liquidity access and employee buying at IPO
summary_extended: Baker pushes back on a reported hedge fund short thesis that anticipated heavy selling pressure in SpaceX following its IPO due to lockup expiration. He argues this thesis is structurally flawed because SpaceX employees and investors have had semi-annual liquidity windows for approximately 10 years, meaning there is minimal pent-up selling pressure that a lockup expiry would normally unleash. He further notes that nearly half of SpaceX employees purchased shares at the IPO price, signaling strong internal conviction and reducing the likelihood of immediate post-lockup selling. Baker adds that he personally is not a seller, and characterizes many large holders similarly, suggesting the short thesis underestimates the bullish alignment of the shareholder base. The investment implication is that the lockup-driven short is likely to fail, and SpaceX shares may hold up or appreciate post-lockup rather than experiencing the anticipated selloff.
detail: Baker pushes back on a reported hedge fund short thesis predicated on lockup-driven selling. He notes SpaceX employees and investors have had semi-annual liquidity for 10 years, reducing pent-up selling pressure. Additionally, nearly half of SpaceX employees bought shares at IPO, and many large holders (including Baker) are not sellers.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this with personal conviction but acknowledges some selling is possible
quote_fragment: everybody who's a SpaceX investor or employee has had a chance to sell every 6 months for the last 10 years

ticker_audit.reasoning: Virgin Galactic (SPCE) is a separate, unrelated company. SpaceX is private/newly public and has no US-listed equity ticker to audit here. No US-listed SpaceX ticker exists to classify, and SPCE would be a contamination.
